mnemonics: Select In, Route Out: Multiplexers are the data traffic cops of digital design, choosing which input gets to go to the output based on the select signal.
keyPoints: A multiplexer (MUX) is a combinational circuit that selects one of several input lines and directs it to a single output line, using a set of selection lines.

In HDL (Hardware Description Language), combinational circuits like adders are modeled using continuous assignments (assign keyword in Verilog) or concurrent statements (<= in VHDL), not procedural blocks which imply sequential logic.
A full adder adds three one-bit inputs (A, B, and Carry-in) and produces a Sum and Carry-out output. It is the fundamental building block for building multi-bit adders.
commonMistakes: Confusing the data inputs with the select inputs on a multiplexer, often misinterpreting the number of select lines needed (n select lines for 2^n inputs).

Using blocking assignments (=) in Verilog or sequential signal assignments (after a wait or in a process with sensitivity list) in VHDL to model purely combinational circuits like adders, which can lead to unintended sequential (register) behavior and synthesis warnings.
